title: Topic 411: Why Walking Simulators Are Valid: Experience as Gameplay
content:
**ABSTRACT**

This paper embarks on a whimsical yet rigorous exploration of the video game genre affectionately (and sometimes derisively) known as "walking simulators." We argue that these games, often criticized for their lack of traditional gameplay mechanics, are not only valid forms of interactive entertainment but also represent a significant evolution in narrative design and player experience. Through a multidisciplinary lens that combines game theory, psychology, and a healthy dose of satire, we dissect the core elements of walking simulators, from their historical roots in early text-based adventures to their modern-day incarnations in titles like *Dear Esther* and *Firewatch*. We posit that the primary gameplay loop in these experiences is not one of skill-based challenges but of emotional and intellectual engagement, where the act of walking becomes a vehicle for introspection, discovery, and a deeply personal form of play. By examining the psychological underpinnings of exploration, the power of environmental storytelling, and the consumer's surprising appetite for interactive introspection, we demonstrate that walking simulators are not a lesser form of gaming but a sophisticated and increasingly relevant one.

**INTRODUCTION**

In the grand, pixelated pantheon of video games, where space marines battle demonic hordes and plumbers rescue princesses from reptilian despots, there exists a quieter, more contemplative corner. This is the realm of the “walking simulator,” a genre whose very name seems to have been coined by a marketing executive with a profound misunderstanding of both walking and simulation. To the uninitiated, these games appear to be little more than interactive slideshows, where the player’s primary verb is “to amble.” They are the artisanal, gluten-free, locally sourced kale salads of the gaming world: an acquired taste, viewed with suspicion by the meat-and-potatoes crowd who prefer their entertainment to come with a side of explosions and a generous helping of headshots.

But to dismiss walking simulators as “not real games” is to commit a grave intellectual disservice, akin to declaring that a documentary is not a “real movie” because it lacks a car chase and a training montage. It is a failure of imagination, a refusal to acknowledge that the landscape of interactive entertainment is vast and varied, capable of accommodating more than just twitch reflexes and strategic prowess. This paper will argue, with the full force of academic rigor and a slightly arched eyebrow, that walking simulators are not only valid games but are, in fact, at the vanguard of a new wave of interactive experiences. They are a testament to the power of narrative, the allure of exploration, and the profound human need to find meaning in the quiet spaces between the action.

We will embark on a journey through the history of this misunderstood genre, from its humble beginnings to its current status as a critical darling and a commercial success. We will dissect the psychological mechanisms that make these games so compelling, exploring how they tap into our innate curiosity and our desire for connection. We will examine the consumer behavior that has fueled their rise, a behavior that suggests a growing hunger for experiences that are more thoughtful, more personal, and more emotionally resonant. And we will do it all with a sense of humor, because if there’s one thing that walking simulators have taught us, it’s that sometimes the most profound journeys are the ones we take at our own pace.

**LITERATURE REVIEW**

The scholarly discourse surrounding walking simulators is a battlefield littered with discarded controllers and strongly worded forum posts. The very term "walking simulator," as has been noted with the frequency of a respawning enemy, was born not of academic inquiry but of gamer scorn [1]. It was a pejorative, a digital scarlet letter slapped onto games that dared to prioritize narrative and atmosphere over the more traditional metrics of mayhem and high scores. Early academic engagement with the genre often found itself on the defensive, forced to justify the very existence of these games as a legitimate field of study. Scholars, in a tone that can only be described as "patiently exasperated," have had to explain that, yes, a game can still be a game even if it doesn’t feature a single opportunity to teabag an opponent [2].

A significant portion of the literature has focused on the narrative power of walking simulators. These games, it is argued, have "challenged the way in how video games are played, experienced and defined" [3]. They have been lauded for their ability to tell complex, emotionally resonant stories through environmental cues and subtle narrative design. The player, in this context, is not a mere button-masher but an active participant in the construction of meaning, a digital flâneur piecing together a story from the scattered fragments of a virtual world. The act of walking, in this framework, is transformed from a mundane form of locomotion into a powerful tool of narrative discovery [4].

More recently, the conversation has shifted to the psychological impact of these games. Researchers and mental health advocates have begun to explore the therapeutic potential of walking simulators, particularly in the context of anxiety and stress management. The "Safe in our World" initiative, for example, has highlighted how games like *Journey* can provide a "perfect escape from the suffocating claustrophobia of your own mind" [5]. The slow pace, the emphasis on exploration, and the absence of high-stakes conflict create a calming, meditative experience that can be a welcome respite from the relentless demands of modern life. This perspective reframes the "lack of gameplay" as a deliberate design choice, one that serves a specific psychological purpose.

Of course, no literature review would be complete without acknowledging the dissenting voices. The "hardcore" gaming community, a demographic known for its open-mindedness and nuanced perspectives, has remained largely unconvinced. To them, walking simulators are the participation trophies of the gaming world: all feeling, no skill. This critical friction, however, has been a generative one, forcing developers and academics alike to articulate a more robust defense of the genre. The result is a body of work that is as diverse and fascinating as the games it seeks to understand, a testament to the fact that even in the world of video games, the pen is mightier than the BFG.

**ANALYSIS**

To truly understand the appeal of the walking simulator, one must peer into the deep, occasionally murky, waters of human psychology. At its core, the genre is a masterclass in the art of the slow reveal, a seductive dance of curiosity and discovery that plays upon our most fundamental cognitive biases. The human brain, after all, is a pattern-seeking machine, an obsessive detective that cannot resist a good mystery. Walking simulators, in their infinite wisdom, provide a veritable feast of mysteries, from the seemingly mundane (”Whose coffee cup is this?”) to the profoundly existential (”What is the nature of consciousness, and why is this door locked?”).

This process of gradual discovery is, in essence, a form of interactive breadcrumbing, a trail of narrative tidbits that leads the player ever deeper into the game’s world. Each new clue, each snippet of dialogue, each artfully placed environmental detail triggers a small but satisfying dopamine hit, a neurological reward for our intrepid curiosity. This is the same psychological mechanism that keeps us binge-watching television shows and scrolling through social media feeds, the insatiable desire to know what happens next. The walking simulator, however, elevates this experience by making the player an active participant in the narrative unfolding. We are not merely passive consumers of a story; we are its co-creators, our every step a choice that shapes our understanding of the world around us.

The absence of traditional conflict in these games is not a design flaw but a deliberate and brilliant subversion of player expectations. In a world saturated with high-octane, adrenaline-fueled entertainment, the walking simulator offers a radical proposition: what if the most compelling conflict is not with a horde of zombies but with our own thoughts? By removing the external pressures of combat and competition, these games create a space for introspection, a digital monastery where we can wander the quiet corridors of our own minds. This is not to say that walking simulators are without tension. The horror-tinged entries in the genre, such as *Outlast* and *Amnesia: The Dark Descent*, demonstrate that the most terrifying monsters are often the ones we cannot fight. The player’s vulnerability, their inability to do anything more than run and hide, creates a sense of dread that is far more potent than any boss battle.

From a consumer behavior perspective, the rise of the walking simulator is a fascinating case study in the diversification of taste. In a market long dominated by a “one-size-fits-all” approach to game design, the success of these niche titles suggests a growing demand for more personalized and emotionally resonant experiences. The modern consumer, it seems, is no longer content with a steady diet of power fantasies and explosive set pieces. They are seeking out experiences that challenge them intellectually and emotionally, that offer a different kind of reward than the simple satisfaction of a high score. The walking simulator, with its emphasis on story, atmosphere, and personal discovery, is perfectly positioned to meet this demand. It is a genre that respects the player’s intelligence, that trusts them to find their own meaning in the experience, and that understands that sometimes the most profound journey is the one that is taken one step at a time.

**REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES**

To ground our theoretical musings in the fertile soil of reality, let us now turn our attention to some of the most celebrated and debated examples of the walking simulator genre. These are the games that have sparked a thousand forum wars, launched a thousand think pieces, and, most importantly, provided countless hours of contemplative ambulation.

First, we have *Dear Esther*, the game that is often credited (or blamed, depending on your perspective) for kicking off the modern walking simulator craze. Released in its final form in 2012, *Dear Esther* is a hauntingly beautiful and deliberately obtuse experience. The player wanders a remote and desolate island in the Hebrides, while a narrator reads fragments of a letter to a woman named Esther. The narrative is fragmented, ambiguous, and open to a dizzying array of interpretations. Is the narrator a ghost? A madman? A poet who has had one too many glasses of scotch? The game refuses to provide easy answers, and in doing so, it transforms the player from a passive observer into an active interpreter, a literary critic in a virtual world.

Then there is *Gone Home*, a game that proves that you don’t need a remote island or a mysterious narrator to create a compelling story. The entire game takes place in a single, seemingly ordinary house in the mid-1990s. The player, a young woman returning home after a year abroad, finds the house empty and must piece together what has happened to her family by examining the objects they have left behind. It is a masterclass in environmental storytelling, a game that can wring more emotion out of a crumpled-up note or a forgotten mixtape than most games can out of a full-blown cinematic cutscene. *Gone Home* is a testament to the power of the mundane, a reminder that the most profound stories are often the ones that are hidden in plain sight.

And, of course, we cannot forget *Firewatch*, the game that took the walking simulator mainstream. With its stunning art style, witty dialogue, and a central mystery that is both intriguing and deeply human, *Firewatch* proved that a game about walking and talking could be just as engaging as a game about shooting and looting. The player takes on the role of Henry, a man who has taken a job as a fire lookout in the Wyoming wilderness to escape from his troubled past. His only human contact is with his supervisor, Delilah, a disembodied voice on the other end of a walkie-talkie. The relationship that develops between these two characters is the heart and soul of the game, a poignant and often hilarious exploration of loneliness, connection, and the stories we tell ourselves to get by.

These games, and many others like them, are not just “walking simulators.” They are interactive poems, digital novels, and playable art. They are a testament to the creative potential of the video game medium, a reminder that sometimes the most revolutionary act is to simply slow down, look around, and listen to the stories that the world has to tell.

**DISCUSSION**

The unlikely ascent of the walking simulator from a pejoratively-named niche to a celebrated and commercially viable genre offers a fascinating window into the evolving landscape of human behavior and consumer habits in the digital age. The success of these games is not an anomaly; it is a symptom of a broader cultural shift, a collective yearning for experiences that offer more than just a fleeting thrill. In a world that is increasingly fast-paced, fragmented, and relentlessly demanding of our attention, the walking simulator provides a much-needed sanctuary, a digital space where we can slow down, disconnect from the noise, and reconnect with ourselves.

The consumer who chooses to spend their evening wandering through the deserted streets of Yaughton Valley in *Everybody’s Gone to the Rapture* is not the same consumer who is looking for a quick and easy dopamine hit from a mobile game. They are a consumer who is willing to invest their time and attention in an experience that is more akin to reading a novel or watching a foreign film than it is to playing a traditional video game. This suggests a growing sophistication in the palate of the modern gamer, a desire for experiences that are not just entertaining but also enriching. The walking simulator, in this context, can be seen as a form of “slow gaming,” a digital analogue to the slow food movement, a conscious rejection of the mass-produced, instantly gratifying entertainment that dominates so much of our cultural landscape.

Furthermore, the appeal of these games speaks to a fundamental human need for narrative and meaning. We are, as a species, story-telling animals. We make sense of the world and our place in it through the stories we tell ourselves and each other. The walking simulator, with its emphasis on environmental storytelling and player-driven discovery, taps into this innate need in a way that few other mediums can. It invites us to become co-authors of our own experience, to find our own meaning in the digital breadcrumbs that have been left for us. This is a powerful and deeply satisfying experience, one that speaks to our desire for agency and our need to feel like we are more than just passive consumers of pre-packaged entertainment. The walking simulator, in the end, is more than just a game; it is a mirror, reflecting our own desires, our own fears, and our own unending search for meaning in a world that often seems to have none.

**CONCLUSION**

In the final analysis, the walking simulator stands as a quiet but firm rebuttal to the notion that video games must be a relentless assault on the senses. It is a genre that has dared to ask, “What if we just… walked for a bit?” and has been rewarded with a dedicated and growing audience. We have journeyed through the history of this much-maligned genre, dissected its psychological underpinnings, and examined the cultural forces that have propelled it into the spotlight. We have seen that the act of walking, in the context of these games, is not a sign of a lack of gameplay but is, in fact, the gameplay itself. It is a mechanism for discovery, a catalyst for introspection, and a powerful tool for narrative engagement.

To the critics who would dismiss these games as “not real games,” we can only offer a gentle shrug and a knowing smile. The world of interactive entertainment is a vast and wondrous place, with room enough for all manner of experiences. The walking simulator, with its quiet confidence and its unapologetic embrace of the slow and the contemplative, has more than earned its place at the table. It is a genre that reminds us that sometimes the most profound adventures are the ones that we take in our own heads, and that the most important destination is not a high score but a deeper understanding of ourselves and the world around us. So go ahead, take a walk. You might be surprised at what you find.
references:
[1] Wikipedia. (2025). Walking simulator. Retrieved from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walking_simulator

[2] Campbell, C. (2016). The problem with ‘walking sims’. Polygon. Retrieved from https://www.polygon.com/2016/9/28/13088572/walking-sims-what-is-a-game

[3] Watts, R. (2019). This is the decade where exploration did the talking. PC Gamer. Retrieved from https://www.pcgamer.com/this-is-the-decade-where-exploration-did-the-talking/

[4] Abreu, G. (2025). Critical Play: Walking Simulators. The Mechanics of Magic. Retrieved from https://mechanicsofmagic.com/2025/04/23/critical-play-walking-simulators-101/

[5] Jackson, L. (2020). Journey, Anxiety And The Unavoidable Importance Of The “Walking Simulator”. Safe in our World. Retrieved from https://safeinourworld.org/stories/journey-anxiety-and-the-unavoidable-importance-of-the-walking-simulator-by-lara-jackson/
